steps.title: Find the main tension
steps.content: The text says the sonnet is known for strict formal requirements, so it may seem unlikely to allow much variety. But examples by Maggie Anderson and Tyehimba Jess differ in subject, rhythm, and structure. Carl Phillips explains the tension by saying recognizable conventions invite poets to experiment by subverting them.

steps.title: Eliminate narrow claims
steps.content: Choice A invents a contrast between Anderson's and Jess's originality and most modern sonnets being conventional. Choice C focuses on the sonnet's popularity and whether its features seem antiquated to readers, topics the passage does not discuss. Choice D invents a long historical period with little innovation, which is also outside the text.

steps.title: Choose the broad summary
steps.content: The main idea is that the sonnet encourages surprising variety even though its rigid reputation suggests that variety would be unlikely. This captures both parts of the passage: the wide-ranging examples and Phillips's claim that familiar conventions make experimentation especially tempting. The answer is B.
